20: hosai gar epanggeliai theou en auto to nai (hosai gar epangeliai theou, en autō to Nai): 'For all the promises of God find their Yes in him.' The singular Nai (Yes) is Christ's own covenant-faithfulness (pistis Christou in its broadest register): the entirety of OT promise — Abrahamic, Mosaic, Davidic, prophetic — is ratified in one person. Paul uses the Greek Nai and its Aramaic cognate Amen as bookends: to the Yes in Christ, believers add the Amen through him to the glory of God. The liturgical shape (Christ-Yes → congregation-Amen) points to the eucharistic and corporate dimensions of this affirmation.
1: Paulos, apostolos Christou Iesou dia thelematos theou — mirrors 1 Corinthians 1:1 almost exactly. Apostolos (lit. 'one sent') carries official commission; Paul uses it defensively throughout 2 Cor against those who question his authority. Dia thelematos theou (through the will of God): his apostleship is not self-appointed but divinely initiated. Hagiois (the saints, dispute level 2): MKT renders 'church' for the corporate body of those 'set apart' (hagios). The letter addressed to all Achaia — broader than Corinth, suggesting intended circulation.
2: Standard Pauline greeting combining Greek (charis, grace) and Hebrew (eirene, peace = shalom). Charis (dispute level 2): MKT 'grace' — unmerited divine favour as the source of all apostolic work. Apo theou patros hemon kai kyriou Iesou Christou: the double genitive presents both God and Christ as joint source of grace and peace — a high-Christology formula.
3: Eulogetos ho theos kai pater tou kyriou hemon Iesou Christou: Paul opens with a berakah (Jewish blessing formula), identifying the God of Israel as the Father of Jesus. Paraklesis (dispute level 2, appears 10x in 2 Corinthians 1:3-7): MKT 'comfort/consolation'; Greek range includes 'encouragement,' 'exhortation,' 'appeal.' The consistent use creates a verbal network around divine strengthening in suffering. Pater oiktirmon (Father of compassion): oiktirmon translates Hebrew rachamim (womb-derived mercy).
4: Ho parakalon hemas epi pase te thlipsei hemon: God comforts in all thlipsis (pressure, affliction, distress — lit. squeezing). The result clause: eis to dynasthai hemas parakalesai (so that we are able to comfort) — capacity to comfort others flows from received comfort. Dia tes parakleseos: through the same comfort. The mechanism is participation: received comfort flows through Paul to others. This is the theological model for his apostolic ministry of shared suffering.
5: Hoti kathos perisseui ta pathemata tou Christou eis hemas: the overflow logic. Pathemata tou Christou (the sufferings of Christ): not merely Christ's past sufferings but the pattern of suffering that characterizes participation in Christ's mission. Perisseuo (overflow/abound): same verb for both sufferings and comfort — they overflow in equal measure. MKT 'sufferings of Christ overflow into our lives' captures the participatory sense.
6: Eite de thlibometha hyper tes hymon parakleseos kai soterias: Paul's suffering serves the Corinthians' comfort and salvation; his being comforted serves their comfort. The circular economy of suffering and comfort is explicitly apostolic. Energoumenes en hypomone (produced in endurance): the comfort produces patient endurance in the midst of the same sufferings Paul experiences.
7: Kai he elpis hemon bebaia hyper hymon: elpis (hope, dispute level 2) is 'firm/stable' (bebaios — certain, ratified, legally valid). Koinonoi este ton pathematon (you share in the sufferings): the basis of the firm hope is shared participation — not optimism but theological knowledge of mutuality in Christ.
8: Ou gar thelomen hymas agnoein (we do not want you to be unaware): disclosure formula introducing the Asia hardship. Baros hyper dynamin (burdened beyond ability): Paul was crushed beyond capacity — existentially overwhelmed. Hoste exaporethena kai tou zen: to the point of despairing even of life. The extremity was genuine, not rhetorical.
9: Alla auto en heautois to apokrima tou thanatou eschekamen: we had within ourselves the sentence of death (apokrima — the official verdict). Purpose clause: so they would not rely on themselves but on God who raises the dead. The resurrection-God becomes the only viable source of hope — a theological reorientation forced by the extremity of the crisis.
10: Hos ek telikoutos thanatou errysato hemas kai rhysetai: triple rescue tense — rescued (aorist), will rescue (future), on him we set our hope (present). Rhyomai (deliver/rescue): the verb of liberation used for God's OT deliverances. The escalation from past deliverance to eschatological hope grounds confidence in the same resurrection-God.
11: Synantilambomenon kai hymon hyper hemon te deesis: the community's prayer (deesis, specific request) co-participates in Paul's rescue. To eis hemas charisma (the grace-gift granted to us): Paul's rescue is a charisma — not personal strength. Ek pollon prosopon (through many faces/persons): thanks ascend from many because many prayed.
12: He kauchesis hemon aute estin: Paul's boast (kauchesis) is his conscience's testimony. Haploteti kai eilikrineia theou: in simplicity (haplotes — singleness, undivided integrity) and sincerity. Ouk en sophia sarkike (not in fleshly wisdom): he explicitly rejects worldly calculation. Charis (dispute level 2): God's grace has characterized his ministry, not rhetorical skill.
13: Ou gar alla graphomen hymin all' e ha anaginoskete e kai epiginoskete: the letters are transparent. The contrast between anaginoskete (read the surface) and epiginoskete (fully recognize/understand). Paul hopes for complete recognition: heos telous — to the end (completely, or until the Parousia).
14: Kathos kai epegnote hemas apo merous: they have understood Paul 'in part' (apo merous). The goal is full understanding: mutual kauchema (boasting-subject) at the Parousia. Paul and Corinth will be each other's crown at Christ's judgment — connecting to 2 Corinthians 5:10's accountability language.
15: Kai tauth te pepoithesei: in this confidence. Charin (dispute level 2): MKT 'benefit twice' — the planned double visit as grace-gift, not obligation. The travel plans were confidence-based expressions of generosity, not capricious self-interest.
16: The planned double visit: through Corinth to Macedonia and return, then on to Judea. Propemphthena: the technical term for equipping a traveler for the journey. Paul's abandoned itinerary becomes the occasion for accusations of fickleness that require the defense of 2 Corinthians 1:17-23.
17: Elaphria (lightness, fickleness): the accusation against Paul. E ha bouleuomai kata sarka bouleuomai: do I plan according to flesh? Sarx (dispute level 2) — 'worldly manner' in MKT; the literal 'according to flesh' captures the Pauline idiom for self-interested, self-serving calculation. The charge: manipulative double-mindedness.
18: Pistos de ho theos: God is faithful — the ground of Paul's own consistency. Logos (dispute level 2): in context, Paul's communicative word to them — the consistency of his speech mirrors God's faithful character. His word to Corinth was not Yes-and-No because God, whose servant he is, is not Yes-and-No.
19: Ho tou theou gar huios Iesous Christos ho en hymin di' hemon kerychtheis: the Son of God, Jesus Christ, proclaimed among them by Paul, Silvanus, and Timothy — three consistent witnesses. Alla nai en auto gegonen: in him Yes has happened. The Christological move: Christ is the Yes to all of God's promises (2 Corinthians 1:20), and his consistency models and grounds Paul's own.
21: Ho de bebaion hemas syn hymin eis Christon kai chrisas hemas theos: God who confirms (bebaioo — legally ratifies) us together with you into Christ, and who anointed (chrio) us. The chrio/Christos wordplay is deliberate: the Christ is the Anointed One; we share his anointing. Participation in Christ's own consecration, not a separate personal ceremony.
22: Ho kai sphragisamenos hemas kai dous ton arrhabona tou pneumatos en tais kardiais hemon: three participial actions of God — confirmed, sealed (sphragizo — ownership mark, used for goods and legal documents), and given the Spirit as arrabon (down-payment/pledge — commercial-legal term for earnest money guaranteeing full payment). The Spirit now is the first installment of the eschatological inheritance.
23: Ego de martyra ton theon epikaloumai epi ten emen psychen: the most solemn oath formula possible, invoking divine judgment on himself if lying. Hoti pheidomenos hymon ouketi elthen eis Korinthon: to spare them he did not come. The journey change was pastoral, not caprice. The defense appeals to divine knowledge of motives — the same knowledge that will operate at the bema of 2 Corinthians 5:10.
24: Ouch hoti kyrieuomen hymon tes pisteos: not lording over their faith (kyrieuomen — domination-verb). The contrast with opponents who lord authority (2 Corinthians 11:20). Alla synergoi esmen tes charas hymon: but fellow workers (syn-ergoi) of your joy. Te gar pistei estekate: by faith you stand — faith is the stable ground, not Pauline authority.
